concept: Measure / Lay Alongside / Take the Relational Size
description: To measure, the Phi way: cloth against the arm, the doorway against the carried table, flour by the trusted bowl. Relational, body-scaled comparison — not abstract quantification, which the language deliberately resists. Kin to 'sharoi'/'thenoi' (exceeds/falls-short), the verbs of honest comparison.
sound_symbolism: 'ma' lays the thing alongside, 'su' and 'e' settle the gentle judgment. No clicks, no precision instruments in the sound — a hand's-breadth word.
grammatical_notes: Transitive: 'mia wetha masue.' (I measure the cloth.) Against a standard: 'shia sheo pelio wetha masue.' (They measure the cloth against an arm.) See PSYCHOLOGICAL_VIOLENCE_OF_MEASUREMENT.md for why Phi measures this way.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Relational measure compares a thing to a use, not a person to a rank — the only measuring the language makes easy.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: The cubit, the hand, the pace, the bowlful: measurement was the body offering itself as the standard, and it built every barn that still stands.
